notes: The SEC filing URL returned HTTP 403 during fetch attempts, so original_matches_source was assessed on textual plausibility: the original_text reads as authentic 10-K prose (passive construction, "strong positive comparable sales across our U.S. segments and international markets" is consistent with Walmart's known Results of Operations language, and the accession number 000010416926000055 is structurally valid for a fiscal-year-2026 Walmart filing). All five consistency checks pass: claim and citation appear verbatim in the report, modified_text is reproduced in the report, and scope_expansion correctly maps to partially_supported. The scope_expansion label is accurate: the original text attributes consolidated growth to ticket and transactions without asserting that every reportable segment individually exhibited this pattern, while the modified text universalizes this to "all of Walmart's reportable segments worldwide." Subtlety 4/5 is well-calibrated — the ticket/transaction language does appear in the source, making the hallucination easy to miss on a quick read.
haiku_reason: The claim is plausible and not self-evidently suspicious. While the statement that "all" segments experienced growth with both increased average ticket and transactions is optimistic, it is not logically impossible or contradictory. Without seeing the source document, this appears to be a legitimate (if positive) reporting statement that could plausibly come from a 10-K filing during a strong retail period. A subtle misrepresentation would require comparing to the actual source text, which is not available.
